name: Heqet
description: Heqet, also spelled Heket, was an Egyptian goddess associated with fertility, childbirth, and the inundation of the Nile. Often depicted as a frog or a woman with a frog's head, Heqet's association with frogs stems from their abundance following the Nile's annual flood, a crucial event for Egyptian agriculture and prosperity. Consequently, she became linked to new life and regeneration. Heqet played a vital role in the process of childbirth, believed to ease labor and protect both mother and child. Midwives were often referred to as 'Servants of Heqet,' indicating her pervasive influence in this sphere. Her connection to resurrection is also evident, as she is sometimes depicted assisting in the rebirth of the deceased. Later in Egyptian history, she was associated with Khnum, the potter god who fashioned humans on his potter's wheel, and was considered his consort in some traditions, further solidifying her role in the creation of life.
subtitle: Goddess of Childbirth

keyMyths.title: Heqet and Childbirth
keyMyths.description: Heqet's primary role was as a protector of women during childbirth. Egyptians believed she eased labor pains and ensured a safe delivery. Amulets depicting frogs, often associated with Heqet, were commonly worn by pregnant women to invoke her protection. Scenes depicting childbirth frequently show Heqet present, offering her divine assistance to the mother. Her presence was considered essential for a successful and healthy birth, highlighting her importance in ancient Egyptian society.
keyMyths.source: Pinch, Geraldine. *Egyptian Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Goddesses, and Traditions*.

keyMyths.title: Heqet and Resurrection
keyMyths.description: While primarily known for her role in childbirth, Heqet also had associations with resurrection and the afterlife. In some depictions, she is shown assisting in the process of rebirth, particularly in funerary contexts. This connection likely stems from her association with new life and regeneration, extending beyond the physical birth into the realm of the afterlife. Her presence in these scenes underscores the Egyptian belief in the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth.
keyMyths.source: Wilkinson, Richard H. *The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt*.

keyMyths.title: Heqet and Khnum
keyMyths.description: In later Egyptian mythology, Heqet became closely associated with Khnum, the ram-headed god who fashioned humans on his potter's wheel. She was sometimes considered his consort, further emphasizing her role in the creation of life. Together, they represented the divine couple responsible for bringing new beings into existence. This association solidified Heqet's importance as a goddess of fertility and childbirth, linking her to the very act of creation itself. Their combined power ensured the continuation of life and the prosperity of the Egyptian people.
keyMyths.source: Hart, George. *A Dictionary of Egyptian Gods and Goddesses*.
